source_paper_title: Asymptotic Nash Equilibria of Finite-State Ergodic Markovian Mean Field Games
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Cohen_2025, title={Asymptotic Nash Equilibria of Finite-State Ergodic Markovian Mean Field Games}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0478}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0478}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Cohen, Asaf and Zell, Ethan}, year={2025}, month=may }
context:
Section 4.1, Remark 4.6 (page 14).

This paper does not address the convergence of exact Nash equilibria to mean field equilibria. Our method relies on the Lipschitz continuity of \(U_0\), the solution to the master equation. While the structure of the Nash system resembles that of the master equation, connecting the two falls outside the scope of this work. The first author is actively working on this problem, which remains open (see [8]).
title: Prove convergence of exact Nash equilibria to mean field equilibria ergodic Markovian setting
background_latex:
Consider a symmetric continuous-time game with a large number of players, each evolving as a controlled jump process on a finite state space \([d]=\{1,\dots,d\}\). For each player, controls are transition rate vectors \(a\in A_{-x}^d\) out of the current state \(x\), with components bounded in a compact interval \([a_\ell,a_u]\subset(0,\infty)\), and with \(a_x=-\sum_{y\neq x}a_y\). Let \(f(x,a)\) be a running cost depending on state and chosen rates, and let \(F(x,\eta)\) be a mean-field interaction cost depending on the player state \(x\) and an empirical distribution \(\eta\in \mathcal P([d])\) over states.

For an \(n\)-player game, a Markovian (closed-loop) strategy for player \(i\) is a measurable map \(\alpha^i:\mathbb R_+\times [d]^n\to \mathbb R^d\) specifying transition rates based on current time and the current configuration \(x\in[d]^n\) of all players. Given a Markovian strategy profile \(\alpha=(\alpha^1,\dots,\alpha^n)\), let \(X_t^{\alpha,i}\in[d]\) denote the state of player \(i\) at time \(t\), and let \(\mu_t^{\alpha,i}\in\mathcal P([d])\) denote the empirical measure of the other players (excluding \(i\)). The ergodic cost to player \(i\) is
\[
J^{n,i}_0(x,\alpha):=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac1T\mathbb E\Big[\int_0^T \big(f(X_t^{\alpha,i},\alpha^i(t,X_t^{\alpha})) + F(X_t^{\alpha,i},\mu_t^{\alpha,i})\big)\,dt\Big].
\]
An exact Markovian Nash equilibrium for the \(n\)-player game is a profile \(\alpha\) such that for each \(i\), \(\alpha^i\) minimizes \(J^{n,i}_0\) given \(\alpha^{-i}\).

The mean-field game (MFG) limit describes a representative player who controls a jump process on \([d]\) against a flow of measures \(\mu(t)\in\mathcal P([d])\), with ergodic cost
\[
J_0(\alpha,\mu):=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac1T\mathbb E\Big[\int_0^T \big(f(X_t^{\alpha},\alpha(t,X_t^{\alpha})) + F(X_t^{\alpha},\mu(t))\big)\,dt\Big].
\]
A mean-field equilibrium (MFE) is a pair \((\alpha,\mu)\) such that \(\alpha\) is optimal for \(J_0(\cdot,\mu)\) and the law of \(X_t^{\alpha}\) equals \(\mu(t)\). Under standard regularity/monotonicity and concavity assumptions (often called Lasry--Lions monotonicity for \(F\) and concavity of the Hamiltonian), the MFG is characterized by an ergodic master equation whose solution \(U_0(x,\eta)\) yields candidate closed-loop controls \(\gamma^*(x,\Delta_x U_0(\cdot,\eta))\), where \(\Delta_x\) denotes the finite-difference operator.

The paper establishes that strategies derived from the MFG system or master equation form \(O(n^{-1/2})\)-Nash equilibria (approximate equilibria) for the \(n\)-player game, but does not prove convergence of exact equilibria.
problem_definition_latex:
Establish a rigorous convergence result in the infinite-horizon ergodic, finite-state, Markovian (closed-loop) setting: given any sequence of exact Markovian Nash equilibria \(\alpha^{(n)}\) for the \(n\)-player game, prove (or characterize conditions under which) the associated equilibrium objects converge, as \(n\to\infty\), to a mean-field equilibrium \((\alpha,\mu)\) of the limiting ergodic mean field game (equivalently, to the solution of the corresponding ergodic MFG system/master equation).

Concretely, this asks to connect the \(n\)-player Nash system (the coupled optimality conditions/value functions for the \(n\)-player Markovian equilibrium) with the mean-field limit described by the ergodic master equation solution \(U_0\), in a way that yields convergence of equilibrium strategies and/or equilibrium value levels (and not merely the existence of asymptotic \(\varepsilon_n\)-Nash equilibria with \(\varepsilon_n\to 0\)).
latex:

open_problem_quote: This paper does not address the convergence of exact Nash equilibria to mean field equilibria. Our method relies on the Lipschitz continuity of \(U_0\), the solution to the master equation. While the structure of the Nash system resembles that of the master equation, connecting the two falls outside the scope of this work. The first author is actively working on this problem, which remains open (see [8]).
subject_classification: mean field games; stochastic control; Nash equilibrium limits
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is a broad convergence problem rather than a crisp finite optimization claim, and failure may depend on subtle equilibrium selection issues in infinite-horizon closed-loop games. While specific counterexamples may exist (as in related ergodic differential game examples), automated instance search would require encoding and solving exact Markovian Nash equilibria for many games, which is computationally heavy and not straightforward to verify at scale.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: There is no simple programmatic fitness signal for candidate proofs or algorithms because verifying convergence of exact Nash equilibria requires solving coupled dynamic programming/Nash systems and proving limiting arguments. Automatic evaluation would hinge on reliably computing exact equilibria and their limits across many instances, which is generally infeasible for large \(n\) and lacks a clear automated correctness oracle.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
